title: Secondary Service Connection: The Claims Strategy Most Veterans Miss
excerpt: Your service-connected condition may entitle you to additional ratings for secondary conditions. Here's the 38 CFR 3.310 playbook -- causation, aggravation, and the pairings that win.
author: Chris Combs
summary: Secondary service connection allows veterans to claim new disabilities that are caused or aggravated by an existing service-connected condition. This strategy is one of the most effective ways to reach a higher combined rating by linking conditions like migraines or sleep apnea to primary orthopedic or mental health disabilities.
keyTakeaway: Secondary service connection under 38 CFR 3.310 lets you claim conditions caused or worsened by an existing service-connected disability -- and most veterans never file these claims.
whyItMatters: Secondary claims are one of the fastest paths to a higher combined rating. Common pairings like PTSD-to-sleep apnea or back pain-to-radiculopathy are well-established in BVA case law.
whatThisMeans: Review your service-connected conditions and identify any secondary conditions you have not yet claimed. Get a nexus opinion linking the secondary condition to your primary disability.
body:
Thousands of veterans are leaving ratings on the table because nobody told them about secondary service connection. You already fought to get your primary condition service-connected. What you might not realize is that primary condition -- whether it's PTSD, a back injury, diabetes, or anything else -- can be the legal foundation for connecting additional disabilities you've developed since.

Secondary service connection under 38 CFR 3.310 is one of the most powerful and underutilized tools in veterans' benefits law. It doesn't require proving that a new condition happened in service. It requires proving that your already service-connected condition either caused or made worse a secondary condition. That's a fundamentally different -- and often easier -- claim to win.

## :gavel: The Legal Framework: 38 CFR 3.310

38 CFR 3.310 is the regulation that governs secondary service connection. It has two distinct prongs, and understanding the difference between them is critical to building your claim.

### Causation (3.310(a))

Subsection (a) covers direct causation. It states that a disability which is 'proximately due to or the result of a service-connected disease or injury shall be service connected.' In plain English: if your service-connected condition caused a new condition, that new condition gets service-connected too.

The standard here is the same 'at least as likely as not' threshold used in direct service connection claims. You need a medical opinion stating that it's at least as likely as not (50% or greater probability) that your service-connected condition caused the secondary condition.

### Aggravation (3.310(b))

Subsection (b) is where it gets interesting -- and where most veterans and even some VSOs drop the ball. This prong covers situations where your service-connected condition didn't cause the secondary condition but made it permanently worse beyond its natural progression.

The landmark case here is *Allen v. Brown*, 7 Vet. App. 439 (1995). The Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims held that when a service-connected disability aggravates a nonservice-connected disability, the veteran is entitled to compensation for the degree of disability over and above the degree of disability existing prior to the aggravation.

This means even if your secondary condition has a completely independent cause -- say, you developed hypertension from genetics and diet -- if your service-connected PTSD medications or the stress of chronic pain made that hypertension measurably worse, you can get compensation for the worsening.

> [!important]
> There are TWO paths to secondary service connection: causation (your SC condition caused the new condition) and aggravation (your SC condition made an existing condition permanently worse). Don't overlook aggravation -- it's often the stronger claim when the secondary condition has multiple potential causes.

### The Baseline Requirement

For aggravation claims under 3.310(b), VA requires establishing a 'baseline' level of severity for the nonservice-connected condition before the aggravation occurred. Your compensation is limited to the increase in disability above that baseline. This is why your medical evidence needs to document both the pre-aggravation severity and the current severity. Without a clear baseline, the Board may grant service connection but assign a lower rating than you deserve.

## :search: Common Secondary Condition Pairings That Win

Certain secondary connections have strong medical and legal support. These pairings show up repeatedly in BVA grant decisions because the medical literature backs them up and the Board has a track record of finding them persuasive.

### PTSD and Sleep Apnea

This is one of the most common and successful secondary claims being filed today. The medical literature documents a strong relationship between PTSD and obstructive sleep apnea. PTSD disrupts sleep architecture, increases upper airway resistance, and contributes to weight gain through medication side effects and reduced physical activity -- all of which contribute to or worsen sleep apnea.

The BVA has granted secondary service connection for sleep apnea as caused by or aggravated by PTSD in numerous decisions. Your nexus letter should cite the documented relationship between PTSD, sleep fragmentation, and obstructive sleep pathology.

> [!tip]
> For PTSD-to-sleep-apnea claims, a sleep study documenting your diagnosis plus a nexus letter connecting it to your PTSD is the minimum evidence package. Stronger claims include medical literature citations and documentation of weight gain from psychiatric medications.

### Back Conditions and Radiculopathy

If you're service-connected for a lumbar spine condition -- degenerative disc disease, herniated disc, lumbar strain -- and you've developed radiating pain, numbness, or weakness in your legs, that's likely radiculopathy. Radiculopathy is a textbook secondary condition because it's directly caused by nerve compression from the spinal condition.

The diagnostic codes are separate: your back is rated under Diagnostic Code 5242 (degenerative arthritis of the spine) or similar, while radiculopathy is rated under Diagnostic Codes 8520-8530 depending on which nerve is affected. The sciatic nerve (DC 8520) is the most common. Each leg is rated separately, and both can qualify -- which also triggers the bilateral factor under 38 CFR 4.26.

### Diabetes and Peripheral Neuropathy

Veterans service-connected for diabetes mellitus (often through Agent Orange presumptive connection under 38 CFR 3.309(e)) frequently develop peripheral neuropathy in their extremities. Diabetic neuropathy is one of the most well-established secondary connections in medicine. The causal pathway is clear: elevated blood sugar damages peripheral nerves over time.

Each extremity is rated separately under Diagnostic Codes 8520-8599, meaning a veteran with diabetic neuropathy in all four extremities can receive four additional ratings -- all secondary to the diabetes. Combined with the bilateral factor, this can produce a significant jump in the combined rating.

### Orthopedic Conditions and Mental Health

Chronic pain from service-connected orthopedic conditions frequently causes or aggravates depression and anxiety. The Board regularly grants secondary service connection for depressive disorder or adjustment disorder as secondary to chronic pain from back injuries, knee conditions, and other musculoskeletal disabilities.

The reverse is also true -- PTSD and depression can cause or aggravate physical conditions through mechanisms like muscle tension, reduced physical activity, poor sleep, and medication side effects.

### Tinnitus and Mental Health

Service-connected tinnitus -- one of the most commonly rated VA disabilities -- can be the basis for secondary claims for anxiety, depression, and sleep disturbance. The constant ringing creates chronic stress, interferes with concentration, and disrupts sleep. BVA decisions have granted secondary mental health conditions based on tinnitus when supported by adequate nexus opinions.

### Knee Conditions and Hip/Ankle Problems

A service-connected knee disability often causes altered gait mechanics. When you favor one leg, the hip and ankle on both the affected and compensating sides take additional stress. The BVA has granted secondary service connection for contralateral knee conditions, hip conditions, and ankle conditions based on gait disturbance caused by a primary knee disability.

> [!warning]
> Not every condition that coexists with a service-connected disability qualifies for secondary connection. You need a medical nexus opinion establishing either causation or aggravation -- correlation alone isn't enough. The Board requires a reasoned medical explanation of the mechanism connecting the two conditions.

## :shield: Building the Evidence Package

Secondary service connection claims live or die on the medical evidence. Here's what you need.

### The Nexus Letter

Your nexus letter for a secondary claim must specifically address 38 CFR 3.310 -- not 3.303 (direct service connection). The opinion should state one of the following:

- **For causation:** 'It is at least as likely as not that [secondary condition] is proximately due to or the result of the veteran's service-connected [primary condition].'
- **For aggravation:** 'It is at least as likely as not that the veteran's service-connected [primary condition] aggravated [secondary condition] beyond its natural progression. The baseline level of severity prior to aggravation was [X], and the current level of severity is [Y].'

The aggravation opinion MUST establish a baseline. Without it, even if the Board grants service connection, they may assign a baseline that minimizes your compensable rating. Give them the baseline on a platter.

### Medical Records Showing the Connection

Treatment records that document the timeline are powerful corroborating evidence. If your primary care doctor's notes mention that your back pain is causing leg symptoms, or that your PTSD is contributing to poor sleep, those contemporaneous medical records support the nexus opinion. They show the connection wasn't invented for the claim -- it was observed by treating physicians in real time.

### Medical Literature

Published studies supporting the connection between your primary and secondary conditions strengthen your claim significantly. The Board gives more weight to nexus opinions that cite peer-reviewed research. For well-established connections like diabetes-to-neuropathy, this is straightforward. For less obvious connections like tinnitus-to-anxiety, the medical literature becomes essential.

### Lay Evidence

Your own statements about when symptoms started, how they've progressed, and how they relate to your service-connected condition carry evidentiary weight. Under 38 CFR 3.303(a), lay evidence is competent to establish symptoms that are observable by a layperson. You can describe when the radiating leg pain started after your back condition worsened. You can describe how your sleep problems began after your PTSD symptoms increased.

> [!tip]
> Write a personal statement that creates a timeline: 'My service-connected [condition] was rated in [year]. In [year], I began experiencing [secondary symptoms]. My treating physician noted these symptoms in my records on [date]. The symptoms have progressively worsened since that time.' Concrete dates and specifics beat vague narratives.

## :lock: The Allen v. Brown Aggravation Standard

*Allen v. Brown*, 7 Vet. App. 439 (1995), is the case every veteran filing an aggravation-based secondary claim needs to know. Here's what the Court established:

### What Allen Requires

The Court held that 38 CFR 3.310 encompasses situations where a service-connected disability aggravates (permanently worsens) a nonservice-connected disability. Critically, the Court ruled that compensation is available for the 'degree of disability over and above the degree of disability existing prior to the aggravation.'

This created a two-step analysis:

1. Was the nonservice-connected condition aggravated by the service-connected condition?
2. If yes, what is the measurable increase in disability attributable to the aggravation?

### Why Baselines Matter

The baseline isn't just bureaucratic paperwork -- it directly determines your rating. Say your nonservice-connected hypertension was mild (10% equivalent) before aggravation by your PTSD medications, and now it's moderate (20% equivalent). Your compensable rating for the secondary hypertension is only 10% -- the difference between the baseline and the current severity.

If you don't establish a favorable baseline, the VA will set one -- and they won't be generous about it. Your nexus letter should explicitly state the pre-aggravation baseline and the current severity level, ideally referencing the specific diagnostic code criteria.

### The 2006 Regulatory Change

In 2006, VA amended 38 CFR 3.310(b) to codify the Allen standard but added a requirement: the claimant must establish a baseline level of severity by 'medical evidence created before the onset of aggravation or by the earliest medical evidence created at any time between the onset of aggravation and the receipt of medical evidence establishing the current level of severity.'

This regulatory language makes it harder to win aggravation claims retroactively. If you suspect a service-connected condition is worsening another condition, document it NOW. Get it in your medical records. Establish that baseline before the aggravation progresses further.

## :fire: Filing Strategy for Secondary Claims

### Start with Your Strongest Pairing

Don't file five secondary claims at once. Identify the connection with the strongest medical evidence and file that one first. A clean grant on a well-supported secondary claim builds momentum and establishes a pattern. It also gives you an additional service-connected condition that can itself be the basis for further secondary claims.

### Use the Correct Form Language

On VA Form 21-526EZ (or supplemental claim form 20-0995), clearly identify the claim as secondary. In the condition description, write: '[Condition] secondary to service-connected [primary condition].' Don't make the rater guess your theory of entitlement.

### Request a C&P Exam -- But Don't Rely on It Alone

VA will likely schedule a Compensation & Pension exam for your secondary claim. The examiner will be asked to opine on whether your secondary condition is 'at least as likely as not' caused by or aggravated by your service-connected condition. VA examiners issue negative opinions regularly -- especially on less obvious connections.

That's why your private nexus letter matters so much. If the C&P examiner says 'less likely than not' but your private specialist says 'at least as likely as not' with a detailed rationale citing medical literature, the Board must weigh both opinions. The more thorough opinion wins. This principle was established in *Nieves-Rodriguez v. Peake*, 22 Vet. App. 295 (2008) -- the probative value of a medical opinion comes from its reasoning, not its conclusion.

### Chain Secondary Claims

Here's where strategy gets powerful. Once a secondary condition is service-connected, it becomes a primary condition that can itself support further secondary claims. For example:

- Service-connected PTSD causes sleep apnea (secondary)
- Service-connected sleep apnea causes hypertension (secondary to secondary)
- Service-connected hypertension causes kidney disease (secondary to secondary to secondary)

Each link in the chain requires its own nexus opinion, but the chain is legally valid as long as each individual connection is supported by competent medical evidence. The Board has granted claims multiple links down the chain.

> [!important]
> Secondary claims can be chained. If your SC condition caused Condition B, and Condition B caused Condition C, you can claim C as secondary to B. Each link needs its own nexus opinion, but the legal theory is well-established. Think of your service-connected conditions as a foundation -- every new grant expands what you can build on.

## :chart: Impact on Your Combined Rating

Secondary claims are often the most efficient way to increase your combined rating because they leverage existing service connection. You've already proven the primary condition is related to service. Now you just need to prove the medical connection between conditions -- a much lower bar than connecting something entirely new back to your military service from years or decades ago.

Remember VA math from 38 CFR 4.25: each new rating applies to the remaining non-disabled percentage. So secondary claims are most impactful when they produce moderate-to-high individual ratings. A secondary radiculopathy rated at 20% per leg with the bilateral factor applied can move your combined rating significantly more than a single new 10% condition.

## :medal: The Bottom Line

Secondary service connection under 38 CFR 3.310 is not a loophole -- it's a fundamental part of veterans' benefits law that recognizes service-connected conditions rarely exist in isolation. They cause new problems. They make existing problems worse. And the law provides compensation for both scenarios.

Your action plan: review every service-connected condition on your rating decision. Research the known secondary conditions associated with each one. Get a nexus opinion from a qualified specialist. File the claim using the correct language. And if VA denies it with a weak C&P exam opinion, fight it with a better one.

> [!check]
> Secondary claim checklist: (1) Identify your strongest primary-to-secondary pairing, (2) obtain a nexus letter specifically addressing 38 CFR 3.310 causation or aggravation, (3) establish a baseline for aggravation claims, (4) include supporting medical literature, (5) write a personal statement with a clear symptom timeline, and (6) file using explicit 'secondary to' language on your claim form. This is how you build a rating -- one well-documented connection at a time.
